dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for traumatic injury at 1223 Tulip Road.
Hello ma'am, I'm Paramedic Ben and this is my partner EMT Sarah. What brought you to call 911 today?
I'm fine overall but my right hip is really hurting and my right toes are tingling.
Ma'am, what is your name and where are you? Can you squeeze my finger right now?
Helen. I'm in my house, on the steps by my porch side.
Do you know what the time is today?
The time... I think it's like six something in the evening.
Ma'am, do you mind if I look at your mouth and see how you are breathing?
Not at all.
Alright, your chest is moving normally, no labored breathing, and we don't see signs of obstruction.
Ma'am, I'll check your pulse on your wrist and feet, is that okay?
Your heart is beating strong right now at 59 beats per minute.
And you're breathing at 16 times per minute - steady and not labored.
Your blood pressure is 116 over 69, also quite stable.
Need to check your sugar now with a small pinch on your finger, can you do that?
Your blood oxygen level is 94 and very good for now.
We are going to monitor your heart with these leads. May I place them on your chest for a moment?
The monitor is in place - tracing shows an irregular rhythm called atrial fibrillation.
Tell me a little bit about what happened before the pain - was it sudden?
Yes, I was walking home. I felt dizzy and started sweating. The next thing I knew, I was waking up on the ground.
Did you fall or did anyone else help you up after that?
My husband was assisted up by a neighbor with a special chair. He remembers about 4:30 pm.
So, the fall caused the injury to your hip - did you hit anything, like your head or back?
No - just my hip. I didn't feel the fall unless I tried to stand right now.
On a 0 to 10 scale, 0 being no pain and 10 the worst pain you've had, how would you rate your pain?
About a 7. It seems like someone is pressing beneath the hip area.
Does anything make it better or worse?
Lying still helps. Movement and pressure on it makes it worse.
Does the pain go anywhere else in your body, or does the tingling?
Yep, the tingling goes from the hip to all the toes.
Got it - we'll keep that in mind while we assess you.
Ma'am, do you have any pain or tenderness in your neck or back, or difficulty moving?
No, I don't have pain in my neck or back and I don't feel any difficulty moving.
Great, and you were not wearing anything that might cause restraint pain, so no spinal immobilization is needed.
We will set up for more monitoring per protocol 4-1 for general trauma management. Is that okay?
I just noted your breathing is down to 12, still steady but slightly reduced.
I need to recheck your blood pressure - it's now 80 over 44.
Blood glucose is still low, but we will continue with protocol as your sugar was previously high.
Your oxygen remains at 95 - well within acceptable limits.
EKG continues to trace atrial fibrillation with no signs of distress.
The ECG has been re-applied and the 12-lead tracing has been replayed and found consistent.
Your pain is still at a 7, correct?
Yeah, though it feels a little less sharp now.
Your heart rate is up now to 86, and you're breathing 13 times per minute.
Your blood pressure has moved up a bit to 104 over 65.
Oxygen remains stable at 93, and the rhythm on the EKG is unchanged.
Let me confirm the ECG monitor again - leads are intact and not showing new findings.
Your heart rate is now 77 and your respirations have increased slightly to 20.
Blood pressure is 120 over 62 and your oxygen saturation is 89.
The EKG rhythm remains atrial fibrillation, per the most recent reading.
We will make sure the ER is aware of your condition. Any preference where you'd like treated?
Yes- to VCU Health Systems Cold.
We'll be using a stair chair to help you down. May I put a safety belt on your waist?
Sure, go ahead.
Now we'll transfer you to the stretcher, secure with restraints for transport.
Everything is safely adjusted - you're secure for transport.
All tight now - you're ready for the ride.
Transferring you to the ambulance now.
Hospital is aware and has been notified you're on the way.
She is folded with hip pain and neurological tingling. History and meds have been taken.
Vital signs have improved slightly from 80 over 44 to 120 over 62.
Three EKG confirmations have shown atrial fibrillation with no new cardiac issues.
No spinal immobilization due to the negative examination.
Pain scale is at seven for the hip and radiating tingling.
Medical history detailed includes hyperlipidema, hypertension, and atrial fibrillation on warfarin.
She is alert and oriented to all four aspects (AOx4).
Thank you for the full report. You've clearly covered key areas.
No changes handled during transport. She remains stable.
Any medication administered during your care?
No medications given. Only monitoring was per protocol.
Got it. Shifting her to the ER using a pull sheet now.
She will be rolled onto the ER bed smoothly.
Transfer is in process.
I have her. No issues during transfer.
That completes the care handoff.
No bruises, rashes, or signs of trauma present on the skin.
Pupils are equal, reactive, and inspection of head is normal. Mental state is clear.
No drainage, obstruction, or foreign objects noted in the mouth or nose.
Heart sounds and breath sounds are normal with no adventitious sounds or asymmetry.
No tenderness, rigidity, or mass palpable in the abdomen.
No signs of urinal distress or bleeding observed.
No deformities, angulation, or sensory loss in the extremities.
Your GCS is 15- all components normal with no motor deficits.
No signs of lymph nodes or masses in the neck, swallowing is normal, and no neck pain noted.
You show no signs of distress or respiratory abnormalities.
Oxygen saturation and depth align with adequate respiratory function.
Skin remains warm with no cold or mottled patches aside from earlier wet attire.
Radial and pedal pulses remain strong with good perfusion.
Initial glucose was high at 218, and we continue monitoring.
You'll be secure during transport with the RAA stretcher and full restraints.
Feel free to stay still and rest. We'll take care of the rest.
Any last questions before we transfer to the ER?
No issues. Just remind me of the initial high reading.
First measurement showed 218 - then dropped to zero. No medications were given.
Ma'am, can you remind the nurse again about the warfarin?
Absolutely - let them know I use it for AFib.
All set from our end. Let's move now.
Transfer to bed is in process.
All ready at the bedside. Coming in now.
Care has now been transferred. Thanks for the support.
Thank you. Feel free to reach out if we need you back.
Using the pull sheet, we'll be bringing her to the ER bed.
Transfer complete. You're under good care now.
Blood pressure has improved, heart rhythm remains stable as AFib on monitor.
No distracting injuries noted during physical exams.
Monitor shows a stable rhythm consistent with AFib on the four-lead tracing.
No significant changes or complications observed during transport.
No changes happened once we were in motion, everything ran smoothly.
All assessments coincide, no new changes or deviations seen throughout.
She remains alert and oriented with no treatment change needed throughout the process.
HR, BP, and oxygen all trending appropriately through transport.
She now receives direct ER care as our task is complete.
She's in excellent condition now, starting at the Cold ER.
No worsening of pain - still at seven, with no intervention needed.
Pain remains steady and manageable as of latest reassessment.
All set at VCU ER with no delays or complications flagged.
